MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Broadspire Services, Inc.'S First Cause Of Action And Fourth Affirmative Defense
Off Calendar Jul-14-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. DARREN S. TESHIMA, Esq. of Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP (Tel: 415-773-5700) for plaintiff Getaround, Inc.;
Atty. MATTHEW J. LEONARD, Esq. of Caldwell & Berkowitz, P.C. (Tel: 404-577-6000) for defendant Broadspire Services, Inc.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF GETAROUND, INC.'s Motion for Summary Adjudication Of Broadspire Services, Inc.'S First Cause Of Action And Fourth Affirmative Defense.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff/cross-defendant Getaround, Inc.'s motion for summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether the parties entered into the express service agreement.

The parties never signed the service agreement. (UMF 4.) Nevertheless, section 14.19 of the agreement provides that it "may" be executed in counterparts. (Leonard Decl., Ex. J [Service Agreement].) Therefore, written acceptance was not a "positive requirement" or "an absolute condition" for acceptance. (See Pacific Corporate Group Holdings, LLC v. Keck (2014) 232 Cal.App.4th 294, 311.) In such a case, acceptance can be manifested by conduct and/or words. (See Estate of Crossman (1964) 231 Cal.App.2d 370, 373 [concluding parties entered into option contract where "both sides for a long period treated the option as exercised, and the contract of purchase as complete"]; Okun v. Morton (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 805, 819 [250 Cal.Rptr. 220] ["Here, the conduct of the parties subsequent to the execution of the 1982 agreement and before any controversy had arisen, is persuasive evidence in determining the meaning of [the contract]."].)

Here, Broadspire sent a copy of the agreement to Getaround with the understanding that it would be executed. (See Leonard Decl., Exs. F, H [Ballard e-mails].) Getaround received the proposed agreement and did not object to its terms. (See Leonard Decl., Ex. G [Wehring Depo., 88:1-14].) After the parties' dispute arose, Getaround requested copies of the agreement and it could reasonably be inferred that it believed that it was subject to its terms. (Leonard Decl., Exs. K [Shanahan Depo., 111:14-112:17; L [Shanahan e-mail]; M [Brennar Depo., 202:4-203:25], N [Brennar e-mail].) Indeed, Getaround asserts that Broadspire failed to comply with its subrogation obligations under the service agreement. (Leonard Decl., Ex. Q [Alderman Depo., 11:15-12:1].) Broadspire established a loss fund account in compliance with section 3.5 of the agreement. (Leonard Decl., Exs. J [Service Agreement]; T [Getaround's RFA response 7].) The parties further agree that Broadspire was paid fees that comported with the agreement. [Leonard Decl., Ex. T [Getaround's RFA responses 10, 11]; Getaround's Points and Authorities, 1:27-28.) In sum, there is sufficient evidence to create a triable issue of fact whether Getaround consented to the terms of the service agreement based on its words and conduct.

Lastly, Broadspire's counsel (Leonard), who is from Georgia, is advised to comply with California Rules of Court 3.1350(f)(2) in citing opposing evidence in any future summary judgment motion. That subsection provides: "Citation to the evidence in support of the position that a fact is controverted must include reference to the exhibit, title, page, and line numbers."

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/RBU)
